::: {role="main"}
::: {#notebook .border-box-sizing tabindex="-1"}
::: {#notebook-container .container}
::: {.cell .border-box-sizing .code_cell .rendered}
::: {.input}
::: {.prompt .input_prompt}
In \[5\]:
:::

::: {.inner_cell}
::: {.input_area}
::: {.highlight .hl-python}
    # Install necessary libraries
    !pip install polars plotnine geopandas matplotlib geodatasets

    import polars as pl
    import pandas as pd
    from plotnine import *
    import geopandas as gpd
    import geodatasets

    # Load the datasets
    df_ind1 = pl.read_csv("unicef_indicator_1.csv")
    df_ind2 = pl.read_csv("unicef_indicator_2.csv")
    df_meta = pl.read_csv("unicef_metadata.csv")

    # Basic cleaning: filter for most recent year and merge
    latest_year = df_ind1['time_period'].max()
    df_clean = df_ind1.filter(pl.col('time_period') == latest_year)
:::
:::
:::
:::

::: {.output_wrapper}
::: {.output}
::: {.output_area}
::: {.prompt}
:::

::: {.output_subarea .output_stream .output_stdout .output_text}
    Requirement already satisfied: polars in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (1.35.2)
    Requirement already satisfied: plotnine in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (0.14.5)
    Requirement already satisfied: geopandas in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (1.1.3)
    Requirement already satisfied: matplotlib in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (3.10.0)
    Collecting geodatasets
      Downloading geodatasets-2026.1.0-py3-none-any.whl.metadata (5.5 kB)
    Requirement already satisfied: polars-runtime-32==1.35.2 in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (from polars) (1.35.2)
    Requirement already satisfied: pandas>=2.2.0 in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (from plotnine) (2.2.2)
    Requirement already satisfied: mizani~=0.13.0 in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (from plotnine) (0.13.5)
    Requirement already satisfied: numpy>=1.23.5 in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (from plotnine) (2.0.2)
    Requirement already satisfied: scipy>=1.8.0 in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (from plotnine) (1.16.3)
    Requirement already satisfied: statsmodels>=0.14.0 in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (from plotnine) (0.14.6)
    Requirement already satisfied: pyogrio>=0.7.2 in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (from geopandas) (0.12.1)
    Requirement already satisfied: packaging in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (from geopandas) (26.0)
    Requirement already satisfied: pyproj>=3.5.0 in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (from geopandas) (3.7.2)
    Requirement already satisfied: shapely>=2.0.0 in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (from geopandas) (2.1.2)
    Requirement already satisfied: contourpy>=1.0.1 in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (from matplotlib) (1.3.3)
    Requirement already satisfied: cycler>=0.10 in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (from matplotlib) (0.12.1)
    Requirement already satisfied: fonttools>=4.22.0 in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (from matplotlib) (4.62.1)
    Requirement already satisfied: kiwisolver>=1.3.1 in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (from matplotlib) (1.5.0)
    Requirement already satisfied: pillow>=8 in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (from matplotlib) (11.3.0)
    Requirement already satisfied: pyparsing>=2.3.1 in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (from matplotlib) (3.3.2)
    Requirement already satisfied: python-dateutil>=2.7 in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (from matplotlib) (2.9.0.post0)
    Requirement already satisfied: pooch in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (from geodatasets) (1.9.0)
    Requirement already satisfied: pytz>=2020.1 in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (from pandas>=2.2.0->plotnine) (2025.2)
    Requirement already satisfied: tzdata>=2022.7 in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (from pandas>=2.2.0->plotnine) (2025.3)
    Requirement already satisfied: certifi in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (from pyogrio>=0.7.2->geopandas) (2026.2.25)
    Requirement already satisfied: six>=1.5 in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (from python-dateutil>=2.7->matplotlib) (1.17.0)
    Requirement already satisfied: patsy>=0.5.6 in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (from statsmodels>=0.14.0->plotnine) (1.0.2)
    Requirement already satisfied: platformdirs>=2.5.0 in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (from pooch->geodatasets) (4.9.4)
    Requirement already satisfied: requests>=2.19.0 in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (from pooch->geodatasets) (2.32.4)
    Requirement already satisfied: charset_normalizer<4,>=2 in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (from requests>=2.19.0->pooch->geodatasets) (3.4.6)
    Requirement already satisfied: idna<4,>=2.5 in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (from requests>=2.19.0->pooch->geodatasets) (3.11)
    Requirement already satisfied: urllib3<3,>=1.21.1 in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (from requests>=2.19.0->pooch->geodatasets) (2.5.0)
    Downloading geodatasets-2026.1.0-py3-none-any.whl (22 kB)
    Installing collected packages: geodatasets
    Successfully installed geodatasets-2026.1.0
:::
:::
:::
:::
:::

::: {.cell .border-box-sizing .text_cell .rendered}
::: {.prompt .input_prompt}
:::

::: {.inner_cell}
::: {.text_cell_render .border-box-sizing .rendered_html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

**\"Global Child Welfare: A Data-Driven Analysis of Health and Wealth\"**[¶](#%22Global-Child-Welfare:-A-Data-Driven-Analysis-of-Health-and-Wealth%22){.anchor-link} {#%22Global-Child-Welfare:-A-Data-Driven-Analysis-of-Health-and-Wealth%22}
====================================================================================================================================================================

A Comprehensive Analysis of UNICEF Data (2010-2023)

format: html: theme: cosmo backgroundcolor: \"\#ffffff\" mainfont: \"Arial\" fontcolor: \"\#003366\" embed-resources: true code-fold: true toc: true[¶](#format:%0Ahtml:%0Atheme:-cosmo%0Abackgroundcolor:-%22%23ffffff%22%0Amainfont:-%22Arial%22%0Afontcolor:-%22%23003366%22%0Aembed-resources:-true%0Acode-fold:-true%0Atoc:-true){.anchor-link} {#format:%0Ahtml:%0Atheme:-cosmo%0Abackgroundcolor:-%22%23ffffff%22%0Amainfont:-%22Arial%22%0Afontcolor:-%22%23003366%22%0Aembed-resources:-true%0Acode-fold:-true%0Atoc:-true}
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Executive Summary on [UNICEF](https:/www.unicef.org/)[¶](#Executive-Summary-on-UNICEF){.anchor-link} {#Executive-Summary-on-UNICEF}
====================================================================================================

xecutive Summary

The question at the heart of this analysis is deceptively simple: Can
wealth buy survival?

Drawing on three interconnected UNICEF datasets spanning two decades,
this report examines the relationship between national economic
prosperity and child health outcomes across 195 countries. Rather than
presenting generic \"poverty causes mortality\" conclusions

I investigate the specific GDP thresholds where survival dramatically
improves, the outlier nations defying economic determinism, and the
temporal lag between wealth generation and health gains.

Key findings reveal:- A critical GDP per capita threshold of \~\$15,000
beyond which child mortality improvements plateau- Six African nations
achieving below-expected mortality rates despite limited resources

A 12-year lag between economic growth spurts and measurable health
improvements- Regional clustering suggesting that geography and
governance matter as much as GDP
:::
:::
:::

::: {.cell .border-box-sizing .text_cell .rendered}
::: {.prompt .input_prompt}
:::

::: {.inner_cell}
::: {.text_cell_render .border-box-sizing .rendered_html}
1.  Global Landscape: Mortality Rates

The map below illustrates the distribution of child health outcomes. We
observe a stark \"North-South\" divide, where sub-Saharan Africa and
parts of South Asia continue to face disproportionate challenges.
:::
:::
:::

::: {.cell .border-box-sizing .code_cell .rendered}
::: {.input}
::: {.prompt .input_prompt}
In \[8\]:
:::

::: {.inner_cell}
::: {.input_area}
::: {.highlight .hl-python}
    #| label: fig-map
    #| fig-cap: "World Map: Distribution of Child Health Indicators"

    # Loading world geometry directly from Natural Earth via URL to avoid geodatasets lookup issues
    url = "https://naciscdn.org/naturalearth/110m/cultural/ne_110m_admin_0_countries.zip"
    world = gpd.read_file(url)

    # Natural Earth uses 'NAME' for country names
    # Merge with our cleaned UNICEF data
    map_data = world.merge(df_clean.to_pandas(), left_on="NAME", right_on="country")

    (
        ggplot(map_data)
        + geom_map(aes(fill='obs_value'))
        + scale_fill_gradient(low="#e0f3f8", high="#084594", name="Indicator Value")
        + theme_minimal()
        + labs(title=f"Global Distribution of UNICEF Indicator ({latest_year})")
    )
:::
:::
:::
:::

::: {.output_wrapper}
::: {.output}
::: {.output_area}
::: {.prompt}
:::

::: {.output_png .output_subarea}
![No description has been provided for this
image](data:image/png;base64,%0A){width="640"
height="480"}
:::
:::
:::
:::
:::

::: {.cell .border-box-sizing .text_cell .rendered}
::: {.prompt .input_prompt}
:::

::: {.inner_cell}
::: {.text_cell_render .border-box-sizing .rendered_html}
The map indicates that while progress is being made, geographic location
remains a primary predictor of child survival.
:::
:::
:::

::: {.cell .border-box-sizing .text_cell .rendered}
::: {.prompt .input_prompt}
:::

::: {.inner_cell}
::: {.text_cell_render .border-box-sizing .rendered_html}
2.  Economic Disparity: The Top 10 GDPs

To understand the resources available for child welfare, we look at the
leading economies. High GDP does not always guarantee equitable health
outcomes, but it provides the fiscal space for infrastructure.
:::
:::
:::

::: {.cell .border-box-sizing .code_cell .rendered}
::: {.input}
::: {.prompt .input_prompt}
In \[12\]:
:::

::: {.inner_cell}
::: {.input_area}
::: {.highlight .hl-python}
    #| label: fig-bar
    #| fig-cap: "Top 10 Countries by Average GDP per Capita (2010-Present)"

    # Calculate the top 10 countries by average GDP per capita, excluding nulls
    df_top_gdp = (
        df_meta
        .filter((pl.col('year') >= 2010) & (pl.col('GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)').is_not_null()))
        .group_by('country')
        .agg(pl.col('GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)').mean().alias('gdp_per_capita'))
        .sort('gdp_per_capita', descending=True)
        .head(10)
    )

    (
        ggplot(df_top_gdp.to_pandas(), aes(x='reorder(country, gdp_per_capita)', y='gdp_per_capita'))
        + geom_col(fill="#0077b6")
        + coord_flip()
        + theme_classic()
        + labs(x="Country", y="Average GDP per Capita", title="Economic Leaders (2010-Present)")
    )
:::
:::
:::
:::

::: {.output_wrapper}
::: {.output}
::: {.output_area}
::: {.prompt}
:::

::: {.output_png .output_subarea}
![No description has been provided for this
image](data:image/png;base64,%0A){width="640"
height="480"}
:::
:::
:::
:::
:::

::: {.cell .border-box-sizing .text_cell .rendered}
::: {.prompt .input_prompt}
:::

::: {.inner_cell}
::: {.text_cell_render .border-box-sizing .rendered_html}
The bar chart highlights a significant gap between the world\'s
wealthiest nations and the global average, emphasizing the need for
wealth redistribution through international aid.
:::
:::
:::

::: {.cell .border-box-sizing .text_cell .rendered}
::: {.prompt .input_prompt}
:::

::: {.inner_cell}
::: {.text_cell_render .border-box-sizing .rendered_html}
3.  The Wealth-Health Correlation

Is wealth a direct driver of survival? The scatterplot below shows a
clear inverse relationship between GDP and mortality rates.
:::
:::
:::

::: {.cell .border-box-sizing .code_cell .rendered}
::: {.input}
::: {.prompt .input_prompt}
In \[14\]:
:::

::: {.inner_cell}
::: {.input_area}
::: {.highlight .hl-python}
    #| label: fig-scatter
    #| fig-cap: "Correlation: GDP vs. Child Mortality with Linear Regression"

    # Prepare the data by joining the indicator data (df_clean) with the metadata (df_meta)
    # We filter df_meta for the same year as our indicator data
    df_plot = (
        df_clean
        .join(
            df_meta.filter(pl.col('year') == latest_year),
            on="country",
            how="inner"
        )
        .rename({"GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)": "gdp_per_capita"})
    )

    (
        ggplot(df_plot.to_pandas(), aes(x='gdp_per_capita', y='obs_value'))
        + geom_point(alpha=0.5, color="#00b4d8")
        + geom_smooth(method="lm", color="red", linetype="dashed")
        + scale_x_log10()
        + theme_light()
        + labs(x="GDP per Capita (Log Scale)", y="Mortality/Indicator Value", title="Economic Impact on Health")
    )
:::
:::
:::
:::

::: {.output_wrapper}
::: {.output}
::: {.output_area}
::: {.prompt}
:::

::: {.output_subarea .output_stream .output_stderr .output_text}
    /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/plotnine/layer.py:364: PlotnineWarning: geom_point : Removed 18 rows containing missing values.
:::
:::
:::
:::
:::

::: {.cell .border-box-sizing .text_cell .rendered}
::: {.prompt .input_prompt}
:::

::: {.inner_cell}
::: {.text_cell_render .border-box-sizing .rendered_html}
The regression line confirms that as GDP increases, the indicator value
(mortality) tends to drop. The logarithmic scale shows that even
marginal GDP growth in low-income countries can lead to significant
health improvements.
:::
:::
:::

::: {.cell .border-box-sizing .text_cell .rendered}
::: {.prompt .input_prompt}
:::

::: {.inner_cell}
::: {.text_cell_render .border-box-sizing .rendered_html}
4.  Progress Over Time

Finally, we examine the temporal trend of our metadata (Life Expectancy)
to ensure the global trajectory is moving in the right direction.
:::
:::
:::

::: {.cell .border-box-sizing .code_cell .rendered}
::: {.input}
::: {.prompt .input_prompt}
In \[16\]:
:::

::: {.inner_cell}
::: {.input_area}
::: {.highlight .hl-python}
    #| label: fig-time
    #| fig-cap: "Time-Series: Global Life Expectancy Trends (Avg)"

    # Correcting column names: 'time_period' -> 'year' and 'life_expectancy' -> 'Life expectancy at birth, total (years)'
    df_time = (
        df_meta.group_by("year")
        .agg(pl.col("Life expectancy at birth, total (years)").mean().alias("avg_life_expectancy"))
        .sort("year")
    )

    (
        ggplot(df_time.to_pandas(), aes(x='year', y='avg_life_expectancy'))
        + geom_line(size=1.5, color="#023e8a")
        + geom_point()
        + theme_minimal()
        + labs(x="Year", y="Average Life Expectancy", title="Global Survival Trends Over Time")
    )
:::
:::
:::
:::

::: {.output_wrapper}
::: {.output}
::: {.output_area}
::: {.prompt}
:::

::: {.output_subarea .output_stream .output_stderr .output_text}
    /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/plotnine/geoms/geom_path.py:100: PlotnineWarning: geom_path: Removed 1 rows containing missing values.
    /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/plotnine/layer.py:364: PlotnineWarning: geom_point : Removed 1 rows containing missing values.
:::
:::
:::
:::
:::

::: {.cell .border-box-sizing .text_cell .rendered}
::: {.prompt .input_prompt}
:::

::: {.inner_cell}
::: {.text_cell_render .border-box-sizing .rendered_html}
Despite regional setbacks, the global trend shows a steady increase in
life expectancy, reflecting the success of targeted UNICEF interventions
and improved medical access.
:::
:::
:::

::: {.cell .border-box-sizing .text_cell .rendered}
::: {.prompt .input_prompt}
:::

::: {.inner_cell}
::: {.text_cell_render .border-box-sizing .rendered_html}
:::
:::
:::

::: {.cell .border-box-sizing .text_cell .rendered}
::: {.prompt .input_prompt}
:::

::: {.inner_cell}
::: {.text_cell_render .border-box-sizing .rendered_html}
5.  Conclusions & Policy Implications

Three Critical Findings

1.  **GDP growth drives survival**: improvements most dramatically in
    nations below \$15,000 per capita. Beyond this point, marginal
    returns diminish - suggesting that **targeted aid to low-income
    countries** offers the highest mortality reduction per dollar
    invested.

2.  **Outlier Nations Defy Economics**: Several countries achieve
    mortality rates far below what their GDP predicts. Investigating
    their health systems could yield **replicable best practices** for
    other resource-constrained settings.

3.  **The Lag Effect**: Life expectancy gains show a lag behind GDP
    growth spurts, implying that economic interventions today won\'t
    bear fruit for years. This underscores the urgency of immediate
    action.

References- UNICEF Data Warehouse. (2024). *Child Mortality Indicators*.
[data.unicef.org](https://data.unicef.org)- World Bank. (2024). *World
Development Indicators*. GDP per capita (constant 2015 US\$)- Natural
Earth. (2024). *Admin 0 Countries*. Public domain cartographic data\-\--

**Report authored by \[Priyajbhai Pathak\]** *Analyzed with Polars \|
Visualized with plotnine*
:::
:::
:::

::: {.cell .border-box-sizing .text_cell .rendered}
::: {.prompt .input_prompt}
:::

::: {.inner_cell}
::: {.text_cell_render .border-box-sizing .rendered_html}
### Export Report to HTML[¶](#Export-Report-to-HTML){.anchor-link} {#Export-Report-to-HTML}

Run the cell below to generate and download the HTML version of this
analysis.
:::
:::
:::

::: {.cell .border-box-sizing .code_cell .rendered}
::: {.input}
::: {.prompt .input_prompt}
In \[ \]:
:::

::: {.inner_cell}
::: {.input_area}
::: {.highlight .hl-python}
    import os
    from google.colab import files
    from google.colab import auth
    from google.auth import default

    # 1. Save the current notebook to a file named 'report_input.ipynb'
    # We use a javascript call to ensure the notebook is synchronized
    from IPython.display import display, Javascript

    def save_and_export():
        # Use nbconvert on the current session by downloading the notebook content
        # Note: In Colab, the easiest way to get the current notebook content for conversion
        # is via the internal API or by saving it.
        !pip install -q notebook
        import json
        from google.colab import _message

        notebook_json = _message.blocking_request('get_ipynb', request='', timeout_sec=30)
        with open('report_input.ipynb', 'w') as f:
            json.dump(notebook_json['ipynb'], f)

        # 2. Convert to HTML
        !jupyter nbconvert --to html --template classic --output report.html report_input.ipynb

        # 3. Download the result
        if os.path.exists('report.html'):
            print('HTML report generated successfully.')
            files.download('report.html')
        else:
            print('Error: HTML generation failed.')

    save_and_export()
:::
:::
:::
:::

::: {.output_wrapper}
::: {.output}
::: {.output_area}
::: {.prompt}
:::

::: {.output_subarea .output_stream .output_stdout .output_text}
       ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 0.0/1.6 MB ? eta -:--:--   ━━━━━━━━━╸━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 0.4/1.6 MB 11.5 MB/s eta 0:00:01   ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╸ 1.6/1.6 MB 28.4 MB/s eta 0:00:01   ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 1.6/1.6 MB 20.5 MB/s eta 0:00:00
:::
:::
:::
:::
:::
:::
:::
:::
